text: It's Dr. Madhu Leba here. I have a case to discuss with you. It's a follow-up from a prior post. I had a patient who was fairly debilitated. I saw about six months ago, presented with obstructing rectal mass.

At that time, I did a diverting end colostomy, and the patient was sent off to oncology for presumed neoadjuvant therapy. Several attempts at biopsy were done and the biopsy demonstrated tubular villus adenoma on three different attempts.

All during this time he was going and getting worked up along with COVID and everything else going on. He kind of got lost to follow up a little bit and then he showed up to my clinic and I had inquired about if he had presumed, had presumed neoadjuvant therapy, and he said no.

I called the oncologist, and they said they could not do neoadjuvant chemoradiation therapy if they didn't have a diagnosis. During this time, he was continuing to bleed. He was very anemic, very malnourished, continuing to have severe problems, severe rectal pain.

In fact, he had difficulty walking, and because of this, I decided to take him for a definitive surgery in hopes that we can resect this and slow down the effects and then get a diagnosis and give him some treatment. This was discussed along the tumor board. However, trying to avoid any further delay in care, I decided to do the above-mentioned surgery.
